summary: Gruber responds to Anil Dash's anti-Substack essay by disagreeing with the characterization of Substack as a Nazi-enabling political project, but agreeing—for entirely different reasons—that writers should avoid it. He argues Substack deliberately blurs the line between individual publications and its own brand, making all publications look alike and pushing lock-in through its social features. He notes that many prominent newsletter writers have already migrated to Ghost, Beehiiv, or custom platforms, all of which offer better branding independence and subscription management. His advice: consider Substack last, not because of content moderation politics, but because better options exist and Substack's goal is clearly platform lock-in.
key_points: Substack's branding trick of making people call their publications 'Substacks' is a deliberate conflation of platform and publication identity, analogous to calling a book 'my Amazon'
Gruber rejects the argument that Substack is politically toxic, viewing it as a neutral publishing platform comparable to WordPress or YouTube
Substack's uniform visual design, email-gate home pages, and Twitter-like social features are all transparent lock-in mechanisms that undermine writer independence
Multiple prominent newsletter writers have already migrated away from Substack to Ghost, Beehiiv, and custom platforms with better branding and management
Substack's own origin story was explicitly 'Stratechery in a box'—replicating Ben Thompson's independent model, then trapping writers in a platform
money_quotes: A publication on Substack is no more 'a Substack' than a blog on WordPress is 'a WordPress'.
They say that publications on their platform are independent voices and brands. But they present them all as parts of Substack.
I really don't get why any writer trying to establish themselves independently would farm out their own brand this way. It's the illusion of independence.
In what world is 'No thanks' a good link to convey the meaning 'Let me read the thing I came here to read'?
It's so transparently a lock-in play that it's almost hard to object to it. It's right there on the tin.
My advice to any writer looking to start a new site based on the newsletter model would be to consider Substack last, not first.
I don't eat at restaurants whose food I dislike, and the food at X tastes bad and is only getting worse.
A free Google font, which says something about Substack.
key_insight: The case against Substack isn't political—it's that the platform systematically undermines writer independence through uniform branding, social lock-in, and the deliberate conflation of publication identity with platform identity.
